questions.question: A valve body ranges from 0.75 in. to 2.25 in. of steel, and the contract requires full coverage with Ir-192. The principal advantage of loading a fast and a slow film together in one cassette is:
options: Both thickness extremes land inside the density window somewhere, from a single exposure and setup
The two films average out processing errors
Total exposure time is cut in half
Geometric unsharpness is reduced by the second film
questions.explanation: The fast film carries the thick sections and the slow film the thin sections in the same shot - each region is interpreted on the film where its density is legal, without extra setups or flattened contrast.

questions.question: Behind the heaviest section of a double-loaded same-speed pair, the films read 1.7 and 1.9. Under typical code rules for composite viewing, this area:
options: May be viewed superimposed at a composite density of about 3.6, since each film also exceeds the 1.3 per-film minimum
Must be rejected because neither film reads 2.0
May be viewed on either film alone without restriction
Must be re-shot because composite densities cannot exceed 3.0
questions.explanation: Stacked films add densities: 1.7 plus 1.9 gives about 3.6 composite, inside a typical viewing window, and both films clear the roughly 1.3 minimum that codes commonly set for each film of a composite.

questions.question: Compared with a low-gradient film, a high-gradient (high-contrast) film exposed at the same energy covers:
options: A narrower thickness range inside the density window - contrast and latitude trade against each other
A wider thickness range inside the density window
The same thickness range at lower density
A range set only by the source activity
questions.explanation: A steep characteristic curve converts small thickness changes into large density changes, so the fixed density window is consumed by less thickness variation - high contrast means narrow latitude.

questions.question: A 0.40-in. Co-60 source radiographs a 2.0-in. section with the film in contact (t = 2.0 in.) at a source-to-film distance of 36 in. (d = 34 in.). Against a typical 0.020-in. Ug limit for this thickness, the setup:
options: Fails - Ug is about 0.024 in., so the source must move back or a smaller source must be used
Passes - Ug is about 0.012 in.
Passes - Ug is exactly 0.020 in.
Cannot be evaluated without the exposure time
questions.explanation: Ug = F x t / d = 0.40 x 2.0 / 34 = 0.0235 in., over a 0.020-in. cap - a classic large-source problem solved by more distance, a higher-specific-activity (smaller) source, or reduced t.

questions.question: With source size and object-to-film distance fixed, cutting geometric unsharpness in half requires:
options: Doubling the source-to-object distance
Doubling the exposure time
Halving the source-to-object distance
Switching to a film one class slower
questions.explanation: Ug = F x t / d varies inversely with d: twice the source-to-object distance gives half the unsharpness - paid for with roughly four times the exposure by the inverse square law.

questions.question: Two 100 Ci Ir-192 sources are offered: one with higher specific activity in a 2 mm pellet stack, one with lower specific activity in a 4 mm stack. For identical setups, the higher-specific-activity source gives:
options: Half the geometric unsharpness, because the same activity is packed into a smaller emitting size
Twice the penetrating ability, because its photons are harder
No imaging difference - curies are curies
Lower film density at the same exposure time
questions.explanation: Specific activity (curies per gram) sets how small the physical source can be for a given output; F drops from 4 mm to 2 mm, and Ug = F x t / d falls proportionally with no penalty in output or energy.

questions.question: In a microfocus projection setup, the source-to-object distance is 10 in. and the object-to-detector distance is 15 in. The magnification is:
questions.explanation: M = (d + t) / d = (10 + 15) / 10 = 2.5 - the detail of interest is projected at two and a half times its true size before the detector's own resolution limit applies.

questions.question: During a tube-shift parallax check, one indication's two images show no measurable separation. The indication is located:
options: At or very near the film-side surface of the part
At or very near the source-side surface
Exactly at mid-thickness
Outside the part, on the source housing
questions.explanation: Image shift is proportional to height above the film: h = b x SFD / (a + b), so b near zero means h near zero - the feature sits essentially at the film plane.

questions.question: In tube-shift depth work, lead reference markers are placed on both surfaces of the part because:
options: The film-side marker proves registration held (it should not shift), while the source-side marker shows the maximum shift, bracketing all possible depths
The markers shield the film edges from scatter
They identify which exposure came first
Codes require location markers on every radiograph
questions.explanation: A film-side marker at height zero must print twice in the same place - any shift means the part or film moved and the measurement is void - while the source-side marker's shift is the maximum any internal flaw could show.

questions.question: Which combination most threatens the long-term legibility of archived radiographs?
options: Incomplete washing that leaves residual thiosulfate, plus warm storage at high humidity
Viewing the films monthly on a high-intensity illuminator
Storing films vertically in inert envelopes at 40 percent relative humidity
Labeling envelopes with job, weld, date, and station
questions.explanation: Residual fixer attacks the silver image - yellow-brown stain and fading - and heat plus humidity accelerate it; archival washing and cool, dry storage around 30 to 50 percent relative humidity protect the record.
